name: Distraction Throw
description: A thrown or placed object that creates a perception event, drawing AI investigation toward a target position without directly revealing the player.
design_notes: Distraction throw needs object identity, noise output, occlusion rules, AI priority, lifetime, and ownership. Designers should decide whether guards investigate the impact point, bounce path, last sound source, or visible object, and how distraction priority competes with suspicious player sightings.
design_purpose: Let players redirect patrols, split guards, or open routes through planned sound events.
Create stealth problem solving without requiring combat or permanent AI manipulation.
Teach players how AI hearing, search, and priority rules work through an interactive tool.
player_fantasy: The player feels clever, using a small object to pull attention away from their true position.

parameters.name: distraction_object_id
parameters.description: Throwable or placed object that owns the lure event.

parameters.name: noise_event_id
parameters.description: Perception event emitted when the object creates sound.

parameters.name: lure_radius_units
parameters.description: Audible radius used by AI hearing checks.

parameters.name: distraction_lifetime_ms
parameters.description: Lifetime before the object stops attracting investigation.

parameters.name: investigation_priority_points
parameters.description: Priority used when competing with other perception events.

parameters.name: sound_occlusion_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule for reducing lure strength through walls and doors.

edge_cases: A distraction lands outside the navmesh and needs a reachable investigation_target_position.
An AI sees the thrown object or throw arc and should react differently from hearing only the impact.
Multiple distractions in one squad need deterministic priority and cooldown rules.
A lure should not override a confirmed player sighting unless the design intentionally allows misdirection.
common_bugs: AI paths to the raw impact point even when it is unreachable or inside collision.
Noise radius ignores doors, floors, and sound occlusion policy, attracting enemies through entire buildings.
The object keeps emitting lure events after pickup, despawn, or checkpoint reload.
Investigation target is stored as client-local position and desyncs in multiplayer stealth.
balancing_notes: Cheap reusable distractions can solve every patrol unless AI learns, cools down, or verifies false alarms.
High priority lure events are powerful because they override patrol, search, and sometimes combat tasks.
Breakable or consumable distractions create stronger planning pressure than infinite stones.
accessibility_notes: Show a preview or after-impact cue for lure radius when the game expects precise sound placement.
Provide non-audio confirmation that the distraction was heard and which guards are investigating.

implementation_notes.unity: On impact, create a perception_event_id with noise_event_id, source position, lure_radius_units, and owner. Query reachable navmesh points near the impact before writing investigation_target_position to AI blackboards.
implementation_notes.godot: Use physics body collision to emit a noise event through a central perception bus. NavigationServer queries should clamp the investigation target to a reachable polygon before AI agents leave patrol.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use AI Perception hearing stimuli or Gameplay Message events with instigator, loudness, and tag data. Server authority should own throw resolution, impact point, and which AI receive the lure.
implementation_notes.web: Represent thrown object, impact tick, and lure radius in deterministic simulation state. Spatial queries select listeners, then AI decision order resolves whether the lure beats current alert priorities.

example_games.title: The Last of Us
example_games.note: Uses thrown objects to redirect infected and human enemies.

example_games.title: Hitman
example_games.note: Uses thrown items and distractions to move NPC attention.
